meta.title: A1 Foundations — Identity, Articles, Verbs & Housing
meta.topic: Complete A1 foundations covering the alphabet, numbers 0–100, articles, core verb conjugations, self-introduction, professions, housing vocabulary, nationalities, and grammar rules including negation, demonstrative and possessive adjectives.

vocabulary.notes: Feminine noun. Most common word for home.
Feminine. Usually a large detached house.
Feminine. Also used for a beach hut.
Masculine. Typically a detached house in the suburbs.
Feminine. 'La chambre à coucher' is more formal.
Masculine. Sometimes called 'la salle de séjour'.
Feminine. Literally 'room of baths'. Toilet may be separate.
Always plural in French. 'Où sont les toilettes ?' = Where is the toilet?
Invariable in colour usage (rouge, rouges — but no rouge/rouge masc/fem change).
Agrees with gender: un ciel bleu / une robe bleue.
Invariable — does not change for gender or number.
Invariable — does not agree in gender or number.
Same form for masculine and feminine.
Same form for masculine and feminine.
'J'ai peur' = I am scared. Literally 'I have fear'.
Both forms now accepted. 'Professeur' is traditionally masculine.
Traditionally masculine — 'une médecin' is becoming accepted.
Same form for masculine and feminine.
Masculine. Also means 'laptop' in some contexts.
Un carnet = small notebook; un cahier = exercise book.
Masculine. False friend — does NOT mean 'agenda' in the meeting sense.
Always used in plural for a set of keys.
Masculine. Always your given name, not family name.
Feminine. Takes l' because it starts with a vowel.
Irregular feminine: -eur → -rice. E.g. Kristin Scott Thomas est une actrice.
Feminine form: écrivaine. 'Écrivain' was traditionally masculine-only but the feminine is now standard.
Preposition. 'Je viens avec toi.' = I'm coming with you.
Used in questions: Comment ça va ? (How are you?), Comment s'appelle-t-elle ? (What is her name?)
Interrogative adjective — agrees with the noun it describes. quel (masc. sing.), quelle (fem. sing.), quels (masc. pl.), quelles (fem. pl.).
Used in questions: Quand est-elle née ? = When was she born?
Standard affirmative. In formal/emphatic contexts 'Si' is used to contradict a negative question.
Standard negative. 'Non merci' = No thank you.
Feminine. Context determines meaning: 'une femme' = a woman; 'ma femme' = my wife.
Masculine: né. Feminine: née (double-e). 'Elle est née en 1960.' = She was born in 1960.
Very common preposition. Used with cities (à Paris), time (à 8h), and expressions (à bientôt). Always carries an accent — don't confuse with 'a' (has, 3rd person avoir).
Used in years: 1960 = mille neuf cent soixante. Important: 'mille' never takes an -s in French (not 'milles').

verbs.notes: Completely irregular — must memorise. The most important verb in French. Note the circumflex on êtes.
examples: Je suis étudiant(e). — I am a student.
Elle est professeure. — She is a teacher.
Nous sommes en France. — We are in France.

verbs.notes: Irregular. Also used for age: 'J'ai 25 ans' = I am 25 years old (literally 'I have 25 years'). Note je → j'ai (elision).
examples: J'ai 25 ans. — I am 25 years old.
Tu as un stylo ? — Do you have a pen?
Ils ont une maison. — They have a house.

verbs.notes: Irregular. Also used to form the near future: 'Je vais manger' = I am going to eat.
examples: Je vais à Paris. — I am going to Paris.
Comment allez-vous ? — How are you? (formal)
Je vais manger. — I am going to eat.

verbs.notes: Irregular. Note the double-n in ils/elles viennent. 'Je viens de…' = I come from…
examples: Je viens de l'Inde. — I come from India.
Tu viens avec moi ? — Are you coming with me?
Ils viennent demain. — They are coming tomorrow.

verbs.meaning: to be called / to call oneself
verbs.notes: Reflexive (pronominal) verb. Note double-L in je/tu/il/ils forms but single-L in nous/vous. Always used for giving names.
examples: Je m'appelle Marie. — My name is Marie.
Comment tu t'appelles ? — What is your name? (informal)
Il s'appelle Thomas. — His name is Thomas.

verbs.notes: Regular -ER verb. Used with 'à' for cities and 'en/au/aux' for countries.
examples: J'habite à Delhi. — I live in Delhi.
Elle habite en France. — She lives in France.
Il n'habite pas ici. — He does not live here.

verbs.notes: Model regular -ER verb. The -ER pattern applies to hundreds of verbs. Endings: -e, -es, -e, -ons, -ez, -ent.
examples: Je parle français. — I speak French.
Je ne parle pas français. — I do not speak French.
Vous parlez anglais ? — Do you speak English?

verbs.notes: Irregular -RE verb (like prendre). Note double-n in ils/elles. Je/tu/il forms end in -ds, -ds, -d (no final s for il).
examples: J'apprends le français. — I am learning French.
Elle apprend vite. — She learns quickly.
Nous apprenons ensemble. — We are learning together.

verbs.notes: Modal verb — followed by infinitive. 'Je dois partir' = I must leave. Irregular.
examples: Je dois étudier. — I must study.
Tu dois parler français. — You must speak French.
Ils doivent respecter l'espace. — They must respect personal space.

verbs.notes: Irregular -IR verb that conjugates like an -ER verb in the present tense.
examples: J'ouvre la porte. — I open the door.
Ouvrez le livre ! — Open the book!

verbs.notes: Model regular -IR verb. Remove -IR, then add: -is, -is, -it, -issons, -issez, -issent.
examples: Je finis à 18h. — I finish at 6pm.
Nous finissons le travail. — We finish the work.

verbs.notes: Model regular -RE verb. Remove -RE, then add: -s, -s, nothing, -ons, -ez, -ent.
examples: J'attends le bus. — I am waiting for the bus.
Nous attendons ici. — We are waiting here.

verbs.notes: Regular -ER verb. Note the common mistake 'regadrez' — correct form is always regardez. The final -ent in ils/elles is silent.
examples: Je regarde la télévision. — I watch television.
Elle regarde par la fenêtre. — She looks out the window.
Regardez ! — Look! (command, formal/plural)

verbs.notes: Irregular verb. Je/tu end in -lis, il/elle ends in -lit (silent t). The stem changes: lir- → lis- / lis- / lit- / lis-.
examples: Je lis un livre. — I am reading a book.
Tu lis le journal ? — Are you reading the newspaper?
Ils lisent beaucoup. — They read a lot.

verbs.notes: Irregular verb. Key exception: vous dites (NOT 'vous disez' — this is one of very few irregular vous forms). Compare: faire → vous faites, être → vous êtes.
examples: Je dis la vérité. — I am telling the truth.
Qu'est-ce que tu dis ? — What are you saying?
Comment dit-on 'hello' en français ? — How do you say 'hello' in French?

verbs.notes: Irregular verb. Note the circumflex on il/elle connaît. IMPORTANT distinction: connaître = to know a person/place (familiarity). savoir = to know a fact or how to do something. 'Je connais Marie' (I know Marie) vs 'Je sais qu'elle est médecin' (I know that she is a doctor).
examples: Je connais Marie. — I know Marie.
Oui, je les connais. — Yes, I know them.
Non, je ne les connais pas. — No, I don't know them.
Vous connaissez Paris ? — Do you know Paris?

grammar.title: Definite articles (Les articles définis) — le, la, l', les
grammar.explanation: Use definite articles ('the') when referring to something specific. le = masculine singular (le livre), la = feminine singular (la table), l' = before vowel or silent h (l'école, l'hôtel), les = all plurals (les enfants). Articles contract: à + le = au, de + le = du, à + les = aux, de + les = des.

grammar.title: Indefinite articles (Les articles indéfinis) — un, une, des
grammar.explanation: Use indefinite articles for 'a/an/some'. un = masculine (un stylo), une = feminine (une chaise), des = plural (des pommes). After negation, des becomes de/d': Je n'ai pas de stylo.

grammar.title: Negation — ne … pas
grammar.explanation: To make a sentence negative, wrap the verb with ne … pas. Je parle français → Je ne parle pas français. Before a vowel, ne contracts to n': Je n'habite pas à Paris. The verb always sits between ne and pas. In informal speech, 'ne' is often dropped: 'Je parle pas' (colloquial only).

grammar.title: Subject pronouns (Pronoms sujets)
grammar.explanation: French always requires the subject pronoun — you cannot drop it. je (I), tu (you, informal singular), il/elle (he/she/it), nous (we), vous (you, formal or plural), ils/elles (they, masc/fem). 'On' informally replaces 'nous' in speech: 'On va' = We're going.

grammar.title: Stress / tonic pronouns (Pronoms toniques)
grammar.explanation: Used for emphasis, after prepositions, or after 'et'. moi (me), toi (you), lui (him), elle (her), nous (us), vous (you), eux (them, masc), elles (them, fem). Examples: C'est pour moi. Toi et moi. C'est lui.

grammar.title: Demonstrative adjectives (Adjectifs démonstratifs) — ce, cet, cette, ces
grammar.explanation: Use to point to something specific ('this/that'). ce = masculine singular before consonant (ce livre), cet = masculine singular before vowel/silent h (cet ami, cet hôtel), cette = feminine singular (cette table), ces = all plurals (ces enfants). Note: French doesn't distinguish 'this' vs 'that' without adding '-ci' (here) or '-là' (there): ce livre-ci (this book), ce livre-là (that book).

grammar.title: Possessive adjectives (Adjectifs possessifs)
grammar.explanation: Agree with the noun they describe, not the owner. mon/ma/mes (my), ton/ta/tes (your, informal), son/sa/ses (his/her/its), notre/nos (our), votre/vos (your, formal/plural), leur/leurs (their). Important: son/sa/ses can mean both his AND her — context decides. Before feminine nouns starting with a vowel: use mon/ton/son, not ma/ta/sa (mon amie, not ma amie).

grammar.title: Prepositions with countries and cities
grammar.explanation: à + city: à Paris, à Delhi, à Lyon. en + feminine country (or starting with vowel): en France, en Inde, en Italie. au + masculine country: au Japon, au Canada. aux + plural country: aux États-Unis. Note: most countries ending in -e are feminine (except le Mexique, le Cambodge).

grammar.title: Prepositions of place (Prépositions de lieu)
grammar.explanation: sur (on), sous (under), dans (in/inside), devant (in front of), derrière (behind), entre (between), à côté de (next to). Example: Le livre est sur la table. Le chat est sous la chaise.

grammar.title: Regular -ER verb pattern
grammar.explanation: The largest verb group. Remove -ER from infinitive, then add: -e (je), -es (tu), -e (il/elle), -ons (nous), -ez (vous), -ent (ils/elles). Example: parler → je parle, tu parles, il parle, nous parlons, vous parlez, ils parlent. The je/il/elle forms sound identical.

grammar.title: Regular -IR verb pattern
grammar.explanation: Remove -IR, add: -is (je), -is (tu), -it (il/elle), -issons (nous), -issez (vous), -issent (ils/elles). Example: finir → je finis, tu finis, il finit, nous finissons, vous finissez, ils finissent. Note the -iss- inserted in nous/vous/ils forms.

grammar.title: Regular -RE verb pattern
grammar.explanation: Remove -RE, add: -s (je), -s (tu), nothing (il/elle), -ons (nous), -ez (vous), -ent (ils/elles). Example: attendre → j'attends, tu attends, il attend, nous attendons, vous attendez, ils attendent. Note: il/elle gets NO ending — just the stem.

grammar.title: Near future with aller + infinitive
grammar.explanation: To talk about what you are going to do, use the conjugated form of aller + infinitive. Je vais manger (I am going to eat), Tu vas partir (You are going to leave), Nous allons apprendre (We are going to learn). This is the most common way to express the future in spoken French.

grammar.title: Gender and nationality adjectives
grammar.explanation: Nationality adjectives agree in gender and number with the person described. Masculine: français, indien, chinois, japonais, espagnol, canadien, allemand, marocain, américain. Feminine: française, indienne, chinoise, japonaise, espagnole, canadienne, allemande, marocaine, américaine. Note: nationality adjectives are lowercase in French (unlike English).

grammar.title: Tu vs Vous
grammar.explanation: Tu = singular informal (friends, family, children, peers). Vous = singular formal (strangers, elders, professionals) OR any group (2+ people). Always start with 'vous' with someone you've just met professionally. Switching from vous to tu is called 'tutoyer' and is usually suggested by the other person.

grammar.title: Interrogative adjective — Quel / Quelle / Quels / Quelles
grammar.explanation: Used to ask 'what' or 'which' before a noun. Must agree with the gender and number of the noun it modifies. quel (masc. sing.) — Quel est ton prénom ? | quelle (fem. sing.) — Quelle est ta nationalité ? | quels (masc. pl.) — Quels acteurs aimes-tu ? | quelles (fem. pl.) — Quelles langues parles-tu ? The actress example from class: 'Quel est le prénom de l'actrice ?' — 'Le prénom de l'actrice est Kristin.'

grammar.title: Verb naître — to be born
grammar.explanation: Naître is an irregular verb used to talk about birth. The key form is the past participle né(e), used with être: 'Elle est née en 1960' (She was born in 1960). The feminine adds a second -e: né → née. To say a year in French: 1960 = mille neuf cent soixante (1000 + 900 + 60). Never say 'dix-neuf cent' for years — always 'mille neuf cent'.

grammar.title: Direct object pronouns — les, le, la, l'
grammar.explanation: Object pronouns replace a noun that is the direct object of the verb and are placed BEFORE the verb. les = them (plural), le = him/it (masc.), la = her/it (fem.), l' = him/her before a vowel. Critical word order: Subject + pronoun + verb. 'Je connais Marie' → 'Je la connais.' 'Je connais les acteurs' → 'Je les connais.' In negation: ne comes before the pronoun — 'Je ne les connais pas.' (NOT 'je ne connais les pas' or 'je ne pas les connais').

grammar.title: Word order with negation and object pronouns
grammar.explanation: The correct structure is: [Subject] + ne + [object pronoun] + [verb] + pas. Example: 'Non, je ne les connais pas.' — the pronoun (les) sits between ne and the verb (connais), and pas comes after the verb. Common errors corrected in class: ✗ 'je ne pas les connais' (pas in wrong place) ✗ 'je ne connais les pas' (pronoun after verb) ✓ 'je ne les connais pas' (correct order)

grammar.title: How to read years in French
grammar.explanation: Years are read as full numbers. 1960 = mille neuf cent soixante (literally: thousand nine hundred sixty). Structure: mille [hundreds] [tens/units]. More examples: 1985 = mille neuf cent quatre-vingt-cinq | 2000 = deux mille | 2026 = deux mille vingt-six. Remember: 'mille' never takes an -s, and soixante-dix (70), quatre-vingts (80), quatre-vingt-dix (90) apply to years just as to regular numbers.

cultural_notes.title: Les symboles de la France
cultural_notes.content: The French flag is Bleu, Blanc, Rouge (in that order — not Bleu, Rouge, Blanc, which is a common mistake). The national motto is Liberté, Égalité, Fraternité. The national anthem is La Marseillaise (originally a war song from Marseille). Bastille Day (La Fête nationale) is celebrated on July 14th, commemorating the storming of the Bastille in 1789.

cultural_notes.title: La France en bref — Key facts
cultural_notes.content: Population: approximately 66.4 million. Currency: the Euro (€). France has 27 regions, 7 mountain ranges (massifs), 5 overseas territories (DOM-TOM), and 10 national parks. Fun fact: 320 baguettes are sold every second in France — bread is central to daily life and culture.

cultural_notes.title: Marseille — Second city highlights
cultural_notes.content: Marseille is France's oldest city and second largest. Key landmarks: MuCEM (Museum of European and Mediterranean Civilisations), Vieux-Port (Old Port), Notre-Dame de la Garde (nicknamed 'La Bonne Mère' — the Good Mother), and the historic Château d'If (made famous by Alexandre Dumas' The Count of Monte Cristo).

cultural_notes.title: Personnalités françaises célèbres
cultural_notes.content: Historical figures: Napoléon Bonaparte (Emperor), Louis XIV (Le Roi Soleil — The Sun King), Victor Hugo (writer of Les Misérables and Notre-Dame de Paris). Modern figures: Daft Punk (iconic French electronic music duo), Christine Lagarde (former head of the IMF, now head of the European Central Bank).

cultural_notes.title: La politesse — The importance of politeness
cultural_notes.content: French culture places enormous value on politeness. The 'magic words': s'il vous plaît (please, formal), je voudrais (I would like — always more polite than je veux), pardon / excusez-moi (excuse me), enchanté(e) (nice to meet you). In France, always greet shopkeepers with 'Bonjour' when you enter and 'Au revoir' when you leave — this is considered basic courtesy and not doing so can be seen as rude.

cultural_notes.title: Les numéros de téléphone français
cultural_notes.content: French phone numbers have 10 digits and are always spoken in pairs: 01 41 12 01 13 is said 'zéro un, quarante et un, douze, zéro un, treize'. Mobile numbers start with 06 or 07. This is why learning numbers in pairs is more useful than individually.

cultural_notes.title: Expression idiomatique — Ce n'est pas ma tasse de thé
cultural_notes.content: 'Ce n'est pas ma tasse de thé' literally means 'It's not my cup of tea' — borrowed from English! It means 'I don't like it / it's not my thing'. Another idiom from the slides: 'Ce n'est pas la mer à boire' (It's not the sea to drink) = It's not that difficult / It's not such a big deal.

extra_sections.label: 🔤 Alphabet & Pronunciation
items: A (ah), B (bay), C (say), D (day), E (uh), F (eff), G (zhay), H (ash), I (ee), J (zhee), K (kah), L (ell)
M (emm), N (enn), O (oh), P (pay), Q (koo), R (air — throaty French R), S (ess), T (tay), U (eu — tight lips), V (vay), W (doo-bluh-vay), X (eeks), Y (ee-grek), Z (zed)
é ≈ 'ay' sound (café, été)
è / ê ≈ 'eh' sound (père, très, fête)
ç = 's' sound (garçon). Used before a, o, u to make a soft 's'
Hard vowels: a, o, u — Soft vowels: e, i
Vowels in French (elision triggers): A, E, I, O, U, silent H, Y

extra_sections.label: 🔢 Numbers 0–100
items: 0–10: zéro, un, deux, trois, quatre, cinq, six, sept, huit, neuf, dix
11–20: onze, douze, treize, quatorze, quinze, seize, dix-sept, dix-huit, dix-neuf, vingt
21–29: vingt et un, vingt-deux, vingt-trois… vingt-neuf
30–39: trente, trente et un, trente-deux… trente-neuf
40–49: quarante, quarante et un… quarante-neuf
50–59: cinquante, cinquante et un… cinquante-neuf
60–79: soixante (60), soixante et un (61), soixante-dix (70), soixante-onze (71)… soixante-dix-neuf (79)
80–89: quatre-vingts (80), quatre-vingt-un (81)… quatre-vingt-neuf (89) — NOTE: no 'et' after quatre-vingts
90–99: quatre-vingt-dix (90), quatre-vingt-onze (91)… quatre-vingt-dix-neuf (99)
100: cent
TIP: Phone numbers are spoken in pairs — 01 41 12 01 13 → zéro un, quarante et un, douze, zéro un, treize

extra_sections.label: 📅 Days & Months
items: Days (lowercase in French): lundi (Monday), mardi (Tuesday), mercredi (Wednesday), jeudi (Thursday), vendredi (Friday), samedi (Saturday), dimanche (Sunday)
Months (lowercase in French): janvier, février, mars, avril, mai, juin, juillet, août, septembre, octobre, novembre, décembre
TIP: Days and months are NOT capitalised in French — unlike English

extra_sections.label: 🌍 Countries & Nationalities
items: la France → français / française
l'Inde → indien / indienne
la Chine → chinois / chinoise
le Japon → japonais / japonaise
l'Italie → italien / italienne
l'Espagne → espagnol / espagnole
le Canada → canadien / canadienne
l'Allemagne → allemand / allemande
le Maroc → marocain / marocaine
les États-Unis → américain / américaine
TIP: Nationality adjectives are LOWERCASE in French (unlike English)

extra_sections.label: 🌍 Continents
items: l'Europe (f) — J'habite en Europe.
l'Asie (f) — J'habite en Asie.
l'Afrique (f) — J'habite en Afrique.
l'Amérique (f) — J'habite en Amérique.
l'Océanie (f) — J'habite en Océanie.
l'Antarctique (m) — Rare usage in conversation

extra_sections.label: 💬 Languages
items: le français (French), l'anglais (English), l'espagnol (Spanish), l'arabe (Arabic)
l'allemand (German), l'italien (Italian), le chinois (Chinese)
le japonais (Japanese), le russe (Russian)
TIP: Languages are lowercase in French. Je parle français. (not 'Français')

extra_sections.label: 🏠 Housing & Colocation Phrases
items: respecter l'espace — to respect personal space
payer les factures — to pay the bills
organiser le ménage — to organise the cleaning
vivre en colocation — to live in shared housing (coloc)
J'habite dans un petit appartement. — I live in a small apartment.
J'aime la musique et les jeux vidéo. — I like music and video games.

extra_sections.label: 📝 Sample Self-Introduction (Template)
extra_sections.content:
Use this template to write your own introduction:

Je m'appelle [Name]. J'ai [age] ans. Je suis [profession]. J'habite à [city], en/au [country]. Je parle [language(s)]. J'apprends le français.

Example: Je m'appelle Sophie. J'ai 30 ans. Je suis professeure. J'habite à Lyon, en France. Je parle français et anglais. J'apprends l'espagnol.

extra_sections.label: 📋 Phonetics Worksheets
items: Worksheet A — Sort by sound (é / è / oi / ou): moi, été, père, oiseau, cou, très, joué, froid, soupe, café
Worksheet A Answers: é sounds → été, joué, café | è sounds → père, très | oi sounds → moi, oiseau, froid | ou sounds → cou, soupe
Worksheet B — Fill in the accent: caf_é → café | fr_oi_d → froid | s_ou_pe → soupe | m_oi → moi | ch_au_d → chaud | s_oi_r → soir | b_ê_te → bête
Worksheet C — Match word to pronunciation: maison → meh-zon | garçon → gar-son | café → ca-fay | oiseau → wah-zo

extra_sections.label: ✍️ Class Correction Drill — Negation + Object Pronouns
extra_sections.content: In class, the correct word order for negation with object pronouns was drilled. Study these corrections carefully — this is one of the most common beginner mistakes.
items: ❌ non je ne pas les connais — WRONG: 'pas' cannot come before the pronoun
❌ non, je ne connais les pas — WRONG: pronoun must come before the verb, not after
✅ non, je ne les connais pas — CORRECT order: ne + [pronoun] + [verb] + pas
Rule: Subject + ne + object pronoun + verb + pas
Positive version: Oui, je les connais. (pronoun still goes before the verb)
Memory trick: the pronoun 'hugs' the verb — it's always directly before it

extra_sections.label: 🎬 Class Example — Kristin Scott Thomas
extra_sections.content: This actress was used in class to practise professions, quel/quelle, and birth years.
items: L'actrice s'appelle Kristin Scott Thomas.
Quel est le prénom de l'actrice ? — Le prénom est Kristin. (quel agrees with 'prénom', not 'actrice')
Quel est le nom de l'actrice ? — Le nom est Scott Thomas.
Elle est née en mille neuf cent soixante. (born in 1960)
un acteur = male actor | une actrice = female actress
un écrivain = male writer | une écrivaine = female writer
